title: Operator-Pending Mode
subtitle: The fourth mode you didn't know about.
summary: Between pressing an operator (d/c/y/...) and pressing the motion, Vim is in operator-pending mode. It's a real mode with its own status ("-- OPERATOR --") and its own mappings.

blocks.text: Vim has four base modes, not three. Between an operator key like {key:d} and the motion that completes it, Vim is in **operator-pending mode** — waiting for a motion or text object.

blocks.title: Why It's a Real Mode
blocks.text:
After you press {key:d}, Vim enters a state where it accepts only motions and text objects. The status line shows `-- OPERATOR --` (in many configurations) or remains blank, depending on your settings. {key:Esc} cancels and returns to Normal.

The op-pending state is a real mode for two reasons:

  1. **Custom mappings.** You can define mappings that *only apply during operator-pending* with `onoremap`. For example, `:onoremap p i(` makes {key:d}{key:p} mean "delete inside parens" — but {key:p} alone in Normal mode (which is paste) is unaffected.
  2. **Motion modifiers** like {key:v}, {key:V}, {key:Ctrl-V} change the operator's character/line/block flavor — so {key:d}{key:v}{key:G} forces character-wise delete to end of file even though {key:G} is normally line-wise.

This is the mechanism Tim Pope's vim-surround and other plugins use to add custom text objects.

blocks.title: The Modifiers v / V / Ctrl-V
blocks.text:
After an operator, you can press {key:v}, {key:V}, or {key:Ctrl-V} *before* the motion to override the motion's natural shape:

  • {key:d}{key:V}{key:f}{key:x} — delete linewise from cursor through next `x` (whole lines, not character range).
  • {key:d}{key:v}{key:G} — delete character-wise to end of file (would normally be linewise).
  • {key:d}{key:Ctrl-V}{key:G}{key:5}{key:l} — delete a block.

Useful when the default "motion shape" doesn't match what you want.
Try `:onoremap il :<C-u>normal! ^v$<CR>` — defines a custom "inner line" text object so {key:d}{key:i}{key:l} deletes line content without the leading whitespace or trailing newline.

blocks.title: Operator-pending is the most important hidden mode
blocks.text: After you press an operator key like {key:d}, {key:c}, {key:y}, {key:>}, {key:<}, {key:g}{key:u}, {key:g}{key:U}, the editor enters a fourth major mode where it waits for a motion or text object to resolve a range. Until that resolves, no buffer change happens. The operator is staged with its count; the resolved motion provides `(start, end, type)`; only then does the operator function execute. This pending state is also why doubled forms ({key:d}{key:d}) work: the parser sees the operator key while in op-pending and short-circuits to 'current line, linewise.'
